title: Respiratory physiology: Airway resistance
body_html:
Flow through the airway's is described by Darcy's law which is represented by the following equation:

Darcy's law:
  • P1 - P2 / R

where:
  • P1 = alveolar pressure
  • P2 = mouth pressure
  • R = resistance to airflow

Airway resistance is primarily determined by the airway radius (resistance is inversely proportional to the radius (to the power of 4) as described by Poiseulle's law) and if flow is laminar or turbulent.


Poiseulle law:
  • –³P = 8µLQ / Ï€r4

Autonomic nervous system
The autonomic nervous system (ANS) plays an important role in regulating airway diameter by constriction and relaxation of bronchiole smooth muscle. These effects are summarised in the table below:

SympatheticParasympathetic
Effect on smooth muscleRelaxationConstriction
Effect on airwayBronchodilatationBronchoconstriction
MediatorAdrenalin (epinephedrine)
Salbutamol		Muscarinic neurotransmitters

Inflammatory mediators including:
  • Histamine
  • Prostaglandins
  • Leukotrienes
Receptorβ2 adrenoreceptorsacetylcholine (muscarinic) receptors


Relevance to asthma

In asthma exacerbations bronchoconstriction may be caused by:
  • Parasympathetic stimulation e.g. by activation of irritant receptors
  • Antagonism of sympathetic system e.g. beta-blocker medication
  • Mucus production narrowing airway lumen

Treatments for acute asthma typically involve medication causing sympathetic stimulation (β2 agonists such as salbutamol and terbutaline) or parasympathetic inhibition (muscarinic antagonist such as ipratropium)
